questions.question: Explain the three main phases of the servlet lifecycle and the methods invoked in each phase.
keyPoints: Initialization phase - init() method called once
Request handling phase - service() called for each request, dispatches to doGet/doPost
Destruction phase - destroy() called once when servlet unloaded
Container manages automatic invocation of these methods

questions.question: Differentiate between ServletConfig and ServletContext with respect to their scope and usage.
keyPoints: ServletConfig - per-servlet, specific to one servlet
ServletContext - per-web-application, shared by all servlets
ServletConfig obtained via getServletConfig()
ServletContext obtained via getServletContext()
Both provide getInitParameter() method

questions.question: Why is thread safety a major concern in servlet programming? Explain with an example.
keyPoints: Single servlet instance handles multiple concurrent requests
Instance variables are shared across requests - not thread-safe
Local variables are thread-safe (stack-based)
Example: instance variable 'counter' incremented by multiple threads causes race condition
Solutions: synchronize, use local variables, or SingleThreadModel (deprecated)

questions.question: What is the purpose of load-on-startup in web.xml? How does it affect servlet initialization?
keyPoints: Specifies when servlet is loaded during application startup
Positive value = load during startup (e.g., 1, 2, 3)
Higher values load after lower values
Without it, servlet is lazy-loaded on first request
Useful for servlets requiring early initialization like database connection pools

questions.question: Describe what happens if a servlet's init() method throws an UnavailableException.
keyPoints: UnavailableException indicates servlet cannot be placed in service
If permanent - servlet will never serve requests until removed/reconfigured
If temporary - container retries after specified duration
Servlet remains unloaded until exception resolves
Other servlets in application continue functioning normally

questions.question: Explain the purpose of ServletContextListener with a practical use case.
keyPoints: Responds to web application lifecycle events
contextInitialized() called when application starts
contextDestroyed() called when application shuts down
Use case: initialize database connection pool at startup
Use case: load application-wide configuration parameters
Use case: cleanup resources on shutdown

questions.question: Why is overriding the service() method directly not recommended in HttpServlet?
keyPoints: service() method handles HTTP method dispatching
Direct override bypasses doGet/doPost/etc. handlers
Should override specific doMethod() based on request type
Allows clean separation of GET, POST, PUT, DELETE handling
Makes code more maintainable and follows design patterns

questions.question: Draw and explain the complete servlet lifecycle diagram. Also write a servlet code snippet demonstrating lifecycle method usage with proper comments explaining what each phase does.
keyPoints: Diagram showing: Load → init() → Ready → [service() loop] → destroy() → Unload
Constructor creates servlet instance (container does this)
init() performs one-time initialization - override for setup
service() called repeatedly - dispatch to doGet/doPost
destroy() called once - override for cleanup resources
Code should show @WebServlet annotation or web.xml config
Include try-catch in init for error handling

questions.question: Design a servlet that tracks the number of hits on a website. Discuss the thread-safety issues and propose solutions to make it thread-safe.
keyPoints: Need instance variable to store hit counter
Problem: multiple threads incrementing simultaneously causes race condition
Solution 1: Synchronize the increment operation
Solution 2: Use AtomicLong from java.util.concurrent
Solution 3: Store counter in ServletContext (application-scoped)
Show code with synchronized block or AtomicLong
Discuss pros/cons of each approach

questions.question: Explain the sequence of events when a client sends a GET request to a servlet. Include all relevant method calls in the correct order.
keyPoints: Client sends HTTP GET request to server
Servlet container receives request
Container checks if servlet is loaded (if not, loads and calls init())
Container calls service() method
service() determines request type is GET
service() calls doGet() method
doGet() processes request, writes response
After response, service() returns (servlet stays loaded)
destroy() only called when servlet unloaded/shutdown
